key_points: The British introduced land revenue systems to maximise income from agriculture in India
The Permanent Settlement of 1793 fixed the land revenue that zamindars had to pay to the British government
Zamindars became landowners responsible for collecting revenue from peasants, but many lost their estates if they failed to pay the fixed revenue
The British revenue policies increased economic pressure on peasants and forced many into debt
Indigo became an important cash crop because it produced blue dye used in European textile industries
European planters forced peasants to grow indigo through the system of advances and unfair contracts
Indigo cultivation reduced soil fertility and limited the production of food crops
The Indigo Revolt (1859–60) was a major protest by peasants against forced indigo cultivation and colonial exploitation
examples: Auction of zamindari lands when zamindars failed to pay revenue
Peasants borrowing money from moneylenders due to heavy land taxes
European indigo planters establishing factories in Bengal
Peasants collectively refusing to grow indigo during the Indigo Revolt
formulas: Permanent Settlement → Fixed revenue + Zamindars responsible for payment
Indigo system → Planter advances loan → Peasant forced to cultivate indigo
Peasant exploitation → Heavy revenue + Debt + Forced cash crop cultivation
exam_tips: Explain how the Permanent Settlement transformed rural society
Mention the relationship between zamindars, peasants and moneylenders
Explain the economic impact of indigo cultivation on peasants
Use the Indigo Revolt as an example of peasant resistance against colonial policies
